God sometimes hides his face and leaves his own children in darkness concerning their relationship with him; and this troubles them more than any outward difficulty. But anxious worries are heavy burdens that believers often place on themselves more than necessary. Sorrowful experiences are sometimes a daily occurrence for the saint; our Master himself was a man of sorrows. It is a common temptation, when trouble lasts long, to think that it will last forever. Those who have been without joy for a long time begin to lose hope. We should never allow ourselves to make any complaints except those that lead us to our knees. Nothing is more damaging to a soul than the lack of God's favor; nothing is more refreshing than its return. The sudden, delightful changes in the book of Psalms are often very remarkable. We move from deep despair to great religious confidence and joy. It is thus, verse 5. Everything is gloomy dejection in verse 4; but here the mind of the despondent worshipper rises above all distressing fears and throws itself without reserve on the mercy and care of its Divine Redeemer. See the power of faith, and how good it is to draw near to God. If we bring our cares and griefs to the throne of grace and leave them there, we may go away like Hannah, and our countenances will be no more sad. God's mercy supports the psalmist’s faith. Finding I have that to trust to, I am comforted, even though I have no merit of my own. His faith in God’s mercy filled his heart with joy in his salvation; for joy and peace come by believing. He has dealt bountifully with me. By faith he was as confident of salvation as if it had already been completed. In this way believers pour out their prayers, renouncing all hopes except those found in the mercy of God through the Savior’s blood; and sometimes suddenly, at other times gradually, they will find their burdens removed and their comforts restored; then they allow that their fears and complaints were unnecessary and acknowledge that the Lord has dealt bountifully with them.
